title: Circular Linked List
description: Making the last node point back to the first, forming a loop -- and the traversal trap this creates if you're not careful.
objectives: Understand a circular linked list's defining trait: the last node points back to the first
Traverse a circular linked list without looping forever
Insert into a circular linked list correctly, preserving the loop

sections.text: In the Singly Linked List lesson, the last node's next was null, marking the end. A circular linked list instead makes the LAST node point back to the FIRST node -- there is no null at the end at all.
A linked list where the last node's next reference points back to the first node, forming a continuous loop with no null terminator.
2. Building a Small Circular Linked List

third.next is NOT null here -- it's first. This is the entire structural difference from a singly linked list. Trying to traverse this the same way as before (while (current != null)) would NEVER stop, since current is never null -- an infinite loop, exactly the kind warned about back in the Loops lesson.
3. Traversing Safely: Stop After One Full Loop

This uses the do-while loop from Control Flow -- specifically because we KNOW the body must run at least once (there's always at least the starting node), and the exit condition is checking whether we've come back around to first, not checking for null.
4. Inserting Into a Circular Linked List

sections.title: Why tracking 'last' (instead of 'head') is convenient here
points: last.next always gives us the FIRST node instantly -- no separate head reference needed
Inserting at the end just means: point the new node to the current first (last.next), then relink last.next to the new node, then update last
This mirrors real circular buffer implementations, often used for things like round-robin scheduling

sections.title: Round-robin turn-taking
sections.text: Imagine a multiplayer game giving each player a turn, then cycling back to player 1 after the last player's turn, forever. A circular linked list models this naturally: 'the next player after the last one' is just first again, with zero special-case logic needed -- the loop itself IS the game's turn order.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Traversing a circular linked list with while (current != null) -- current is NEVER null, causing an infinite loop.
Forgetting the special case for inserting into an EMPTY circular list -- the very first node must point to ITSELF, not remain null.
Losing track of which node is 'first' after several insertions -- tracking last (whose .next is always first) avoids needing a separate head reference.
Forgetting to update the LAST node's next pointer when appending, leaving the loop broken (pointing to null again) instead of staying circular.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the defining structural difference between a circular and a singly linked list?
questions.answer: In a circular linked list, the last node's next reference points back to the first node instead of being null, forming a continuous loop with no natural end.

questions.question: Why does while (current != null) fail for traversing a circular linked list?
questions.answer: No node in a circular linked list ever has a null next reference -- the last node points back to the first -- so that condition is never false, causing an infinite loop. Traversal must instead stop when the current node returns to the starting node.

questions.question: What special case must be handled when inserting into an empty circular linked list?
questions.answer: The very first node inserted must point its next reference to ITSELF, since there's no other node yet to complete the loop with.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A circular linked list's last node points back to the first node instead of to null, forming a continuous loop. This means traversal can't use while (current != null) like a singly linked list -- it must instead stop once current returns to the starting node, typically with a do-while loop. Inserting into an empty circular list requires a special case: the first node must point to itself. Circular linked lists naturally model round-robin scenarios, like cycling through players' turns, where 'the next one after the last' is simply the first again, with no special-case logic needed in the application itself.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Circular linked list = a roundabout, not a dead-end street -- the last node feeds right back into the first. Never check for null to stop; check for 'have I returned to where I started?' instead.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does checking for null fail when traversing a circular linked list?
What special case applies when inserting into an empty circular linked list?
Give a real-world scenario a circular linked list models naturally.

sections.title: Insert into a Sorted Circular Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given a node from a circular linked list sorted in ascending order, write a function to insert insertVal into the list such that it remains a sorted circular list. Return the original given node's reference.

examples.explanation: The list is 3->4->1 (circularly), with the 'wrap point' between 4 (max) and 1 (min). 2 fits between 1 and 3 (across the wrap point), giving 3->4->1->2->(back to 3).

hints: Walk the circle looking for a spot where prev.val <= insertVal <= current.val -- the normal case.
Special case: if you complete a full loop without finding a spot, insert at the 'wrap point' -- where prev.val > current.val (the max-to-min transition), or anywhere if all values are equal.
Special case: an empty list needs the new node to point to itself, exactly like this lesson's insertAtEnd base case.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all values into a List, insert the new value into the correct sorted position, then rebuild the circular list from scratch.
approaches.output: For head = [3,4,1], insertVal = 2: [3,4,1,2] (in some rotation, still circular and sorted)
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the values list and new nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Walk the circle once, inserting at the first valid spot found -- the normal in-range case, or the wrap-point case if no normal spot exists.
